text: Welcome to another video in this series of Versius robotic thoracic surgery from Sven Seifert and his team in Chemnitz in Germany. So here we're doing quite a small case really. This is a right upper lobe nodule of unknown histology. The mission is to go and get it out, maybe look at some lymph nodes and main mission, find histology. So because it's a smaller procedure, Sven has elected to just use four ports rather than the usual five. He's got two Versius arms, which you can see by the purple and the cyan instrument units, top right, top left. And as you can see, he's got really good view, nice lung collapse using CO², but lots of adhesions, poor guy. So happens to us all, but he's doing a really nice job with the Versius system, combined with a good bedside assistant to take down these adhesions.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated grasper in the right hand and a fenestrated Maryland with energy in the left hand to manipulate the adhesions. An assistant introduces a harmonic device to aid in haemostasis, minimizing blood in the surgical field for better camera visibility. A rolled-up swab is used to manage blood without disrupting the CO₂-induced lung collapse.

Sven uses rolled-up swabs to manage blood and prevent air leaks while peeling away loose adhesions with a harmonic device for larger adhesions. The Maryland and fenestrated grasper identify a nodule at the apex of the right upper lobe. The robotic grasper lifts the nodule for stapling, using a Covidian purple tri-stapler fired by the assistant. The setup includes two 5mm ports for robotic arms, a 12mm port for the stapler, and a 10mm port for the endoscope, with the endoscope positioned low in the chest.

The surgeon adjusts the robotic arm positions to optimize movement around the chest, successfully extracting the lung nodule in a retrieval bag through a slightly extended incision. The assistant aids in retracting and performing additional adhesiolysis while inspecting for lymph nodes, particularly in the subcarinal station, amidst numerous adhesions.

The surgeon carefully examines the surgical field, identifying and removing a mid-lymph node anterior to the superior vena cava (SVC), which appears to be a station IIIa node. The node is extracted using the thumb of a glove to prevent potential seeding through the port. The Versius robotic system is utilized, with colored instrument arms visible, indicating their flexible positioning.

Shows that you can do much more minor cases as well as major cases with the Versius. Shows you can just use two arms rather than three if it's a small, quick case. And really nice job. You can use the robot just to position your drain really nicely. And a great job. Well done to Sven and the team making a real success of Versys robotic lobectomy in Chemnitz in Germany. And look out for many more videos in this series. We're trying to do as many as we can just to show everybody how it's working with Versys in the chest.
